Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
Spring 2007

Outstanding 25th WIOL Season

Jim Siscel, WIOL Director

We completed the 25th Season of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) with a great Championship Meet at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on February 17th. The weather was great, the courses were challenging, and many enjoyed a picnic lunch while waiting for the awards ceremony.

The league had 227 registered participants. By the end of the Championships there had been 1084 starts. Both figures set a new record. The top teams were Middle School-Bremerton, JV Girls-EAS, JV Boys-Burlington-Edison, Varsity Girls-Lakeside, Varsity Boys-Thomas Jefferson. The top individuals were Elementary Boys-Mathew Rogers, Middle School Girls-Anne Wilkinson, Middle School Boys-Michael Klinge, JV Girls-Kelly Bauer, JV Boys-Joseph Spencer, Varsity Girls-Tori Borish, Varsity Boys-Huw Stradling.

11 WIOL participants will be participating in the US Interscholastic Orienteering Championships being held near Wilmington, DE on March 29 and April 1. I expect to see our participants at the top of the listings after the championship meet.

Bearing 315 Editor Needed!

The current editor is finishing his promised 4 issues and a new editor is urgently needed. Editorship can be very rewarding and can expand your editorial and text formatting skills! The primary requirement is to have some computer skills. Past issues have been put together using everything from FrameMaker, and InDesign, to Microsoft Word. 2007 Street Scramble season opens April 29 in U-District
by Terry Farrah

Since 2003, Cascade has partnered with Meridian Geographics to put on a series of urban orienteering events each spring and summer. The series has expanded each year, with seven events planned for 2007.

[Image: Black and white photograph of two young male orienteers, Tyler Frederickson and Jake Reeder, standing in a park setting and looking closely at a map. Caption: Tyler Frederickson and Jake Reeder at a Green Lake control (photo by Brian Thompson).]

The season opener is the U-District Street Scramble on Sunday, April 29, at University Heights Center. This has been the most popular Street Scramble of the year, with 288 attending last year.

Street Scramble has a score-O format with 90-minute and 3-hour options. About 35 control locations are circled on a modified USGS map of a town or neighborhood. Each control has a point value. The object is to gather as many points as possible in the time limit.

Because of the risk of theft, control bags and epunch units are not used. Instead, participants answer a multiple-choice question about each control feature to prove they were there. Example:

Feature: Statue, NW corner of intersection
Question: What is the date on the plaque at the base of the statue?
A: 1973
B: 1989
C: 2001

Many Street Scramble regulars are Cascade members, but many others have never tried forest orienteering, even after doing Street Scramble for years.

This year a hybrid urban/park Street Scramble will be offered at Seward Park on Friday, June 22. Some controls will be in the park using conventional markers, and some will be on the neighborhood streets. We hope that by exposing Street Scramble regulars to park orienteering we will attract them to attend Cascade's other events. The Seward Park Street Scramble will start at 7pm.

Meridian Geographics, or "MerGeo", is myself (Terry Farrah) and Eric Bone. We are both Cascade members and, through MerGeo, we earn our living putting on navigation and running events full-time. Besides Street Scramble, MerGeo also puts on a sprint adventure race series (BEAST), a trail run series (Northwest Trail Runs), and, starting in 2007, a trail running relay called Six Foot. Also, MerGeo puts on some Street Scramble events in partnership with other organizations besides Cascade.

There are two reasons behind Cascade's partnership with MerGeo in putting on Street Scramble:

  1. Street Scramble attracts orienteering newcomers who might not attend a forest orienteering event.

  2. The partnership allows MerGeo to apply for grants and sponsorships designated for non-profit organizations. Funds from such grants and sponsorships benefit both Cascade and MerGeo, with Cascade's share earmarked for the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL).

The U-District Street Scramble starts at 10:00 a.m. on Sunday, April 29. Map issue is at 9:30, and check-in opens at 8:30. The start is at University Heights Center, University Way NE and NE 50th St. Pre-registration price for Cascade members is $10/adult, $5 ages 6-21. Day-of-event prices are $5 more for ages 13+. To pre-register or for more details, visit StreetScramble.com.

A full schedule of Street Scramble events can be found at both StreetScramble.com and CascadeOC.org.

WMS Junior High Team Prepares to Defend National Orienteering Championship in Maryland.
Gabrielle Lindquist and Rachel Ellis

In all our years of participating in orienteering events and after-school practices, we never dreamed of having the opportunity of competing in the Interscholastics. The competition has given us a goal. We now have something to work for and struggle to achieve greatness in. Training for this event has also been very inspiring. Every day that we meet with the others in our team, we learn something new about orienteering, and its many different symbols, rules, and tips to complete a course faster. With the competition just one week away, we are training as much as possible, and looking forward to traveling to Maryland and participating in an event that we will remember for years to come.

[Image: Black and white photograph of four young female orienteers standing together outdoors, smiling at the camera. Caption: The WMS Junior Team: Rachel, Dana, Jordan, and Gabrielle (photo by Eileen Breseman).]

President's Comments

Jim Siscel, President

We completed a full four months of meets with the Winter O/WIOL Championships at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on February 17th. I would like to thank the eight course setters involved for great courses. It is not an easy job designing seven courses that will be challenging to all involved. I would like to thank Jana Dvorak, our meet director for all the meets. Jana made sure that all positions were manned and took care of any problems that arose very quickly and efficiently. Thank you to Ben Hall for coordinating the Epunch system and getting the results out quickly. THANK YOU to all the many volunteers who stepped forward to run the meets. Without YOU we couldn't have had such a successful season.

We have had to cancel the first two meets after the completion of the Winter O/WIOL season for differing reasons. The Army at Fort Lewis was very slow getting our permission request finalized even though we started before Thanksgiving. Once we received permission Mike Schuh and I went to the area to upgrade the map. We quickly discovered that it was going to take much longer than anticipated and it couldn't be done before the meet. Will we have a meet at Fort Lewis in the future? That is our hope, but there is one concern that has to be cleared up before we can allow our youth to participate. I will be working the Fort Lewis personnel to hopefully fix this concern.

Our second meet was cancelled, Night O at Lincoln Park, because we did not have a Meet Director or Course Setter within the time period that the board had determined to cancel a meet. We did not like having to cancel the meet. We need people to step forward and volunteer for these two positions. We have people who will help and mentor any volunteers for these positions. Please volunteer. The meet director directions are on the webpage and the course setter requirements will be sent to whoever volunteers. Because two meets were cancelled we had to cancel the Ultimate O Series as well. We hope that we do not have to cancel any more of our spring meets. We are working on land permission for all the meets. Check the website and as soon as we receive it the "pending land permission" will be removed and you will know the meet will commence. Of course we still have to have a Meet Director and Course Setter.

The Cell Phone O that was run by our young orienteers as a benefit for the WIOL participants going to the US Interscholastic Championships was a great success. Hamlin Park with its many trails was a challenge. "Which way do I turn? How many trails do I pass? Are YOU SURE, I don't see anything like that! Tennis Courts, you really shouldn't be over there, turn around." The two benefits, Hyak Lake and Hamlin, raised $445 for the participants. We are planning on having another Cell Phone O in the fall.

A reminder that whistles are required to be carried by all individuals and one individual in a group at all Cascade meets. We will provide you with one free whistle, but if you leave it at home we will ask for a $1 donation.

The board decided that we needed to get the Appreciation Coupons to our volunteers quicker. So the new procedure is that after you work for 1½ hours at a meet you are to go see the Meet Director who will issue you your Appreciation Coupon. This way if you work the early shift you can use your coupon that day. If you work the late shift you can use your coupon at the next meet. We will not be mailing Appreciation Coupons out any longer. I would like to thank Heidi Cusworth who has been our Appreciation Coupon Coordinator for the last few years for doing a great job.

Don Denbo has completed his agreed upon four issues of Bearing 315. We need a new editor. There are four issues a year. Don will help you get started. Please contact me or Don if you are interested in this position.

Please remember that volunteers are what make this club run. If you enjoy orienteering please volunteer at some meets during the year.

Tips & Techniques

Route Selections

If you are a competitive orienteer a good thing to do after each meet is to mark your map showing your route selection between each control. This enables you to compare and discuss route selection with other orienteers who ran the same course, thereby analyzing your route selection. As you share experiences with other orienteers you will pick up new techniques and tactics and improve your own route selections. This is a good way to keep your mind thinking about route choices between meets and allows you to "run" a course any time you want. (February/March 1981 Bearing 315)

Practical marker hanging for course setters: A guide explaining how we do it.

© 2003 Harvey Friedman

I have not found this information written anywhere and think it could be useful in inducing orienteers who have not previously to try setting courses.

This is not about course design for there are many sources available discussing how to set courses and what the requirements are for the various age/sex classes. This writeup assumes that courses have been designed 2 weeks before a meet and suggests efficient ways of having them vetted and hanging the markers with minimal effort.

  1. Using bright neon surveyor's tape, put a foot-long piece at the control site with the course and control number listed. For example, course 3F, #4 and course 4, #8. This allows the course vetter (at her convenience) to check all the locations in the field (not just on paper) with enough time to make suggestions for changes when necessary.
  2. After the course vetter has approved the courses, get the control markers as soon as they are available. Assign control marker codes to each of the controls on the courses.
  3. Prepare a master map putting all the courses' control circles on the single map with the control marker codes (e.g., 106) at the corresponding circle.
  4. Divide up the map into sectors relative to where one would park vehicle when hanging, with perhaps 10-12 markers in each sector. For large areas where there are few controls, the sector may contain only 3 for example.
  5. For each of these sectors, plan a route as you might for score-O or for a rogaine. The key is expending as little energy as you can while hanging the markers on each route. Returning to your vehicle to reload with the markers for the next sector is part of the plan.
  6. In course order, make a master punch check list. You don't have to actually punch a punch card but can merely copy dots on a sheet of paper for each. These lists will go to the finish/checking crew.
  7. Take the control markers and arrange them according to how each sector route resulted. I use plastic grocery bags to keep each sector separate. This is best done the night before you intend to actually hang the markers.

Now assume we are at morning (early) of the meet.

  1. Taking a) your list of course controls - control codes and b) your master map hang the markers in each sector. This will be easy because by sorting them in bags and having surveyor's tape in the field, a quick look at the master map assures you that you have the correct one at each control point.
  2. By returning to vehicle to reload with next sector's markers, you can assure any early-arriving meet staff that things are going well.
  3. After all markers are set out, have course vetter (or prerunners) run each course.
  4. Fuel up with the breakfast that you missed. Congratulate yourself for your hard work and prepare to field complaints from disgruntled competitors who won't blame themselves for their problems but must blame SOMEONE.

This procedure works well for standard local meets. Even a slow Oer such as I used to be able (before for my foot problem) to set out the 30 or so markers for a local C meet in time for the start. Naturally for other types of meets, one must adapt.

For A (national or international) meets, using a course controller's master control sheet such as we used for the 1997 A meet at Trollhaugen helps keep the course design team informed of who did what when. I believe that Don Denbo has copy of this sheet on his computer if anyone wants to see blank one.

When using electronic check systems such as SportIdent or Emit, step 6 above may be omitted unless one is super paranoid. Instead, one must program the electronic markers to start at the correct time with their own IDs for the meet. Would be good idea to make sure batteries are all good as well.

Going into more detail about using either SportIdent or Emit is beyond the scope of this guide.

Finally, there is matter of control pickup. At small parks, I found it easier to use sector technique myself.

With lots of volunteer help with control pickup, it is essential that someone be in charge of checking off the retrieved markers making sure that none were missed. Sorting them numerically into the storage box is a good way to double check that none were missed. Even in rainy times, it is best not to leave until all have been retrieved.

I hope this takes the mystery out of course design/control marker hanging and encourages new setters to give it a try.

Orienteer Ratings of Cascade OC

Mark Hartinger (President-1984), updated by Jim Siscel-2007

Super First Class Orienteer

  • Far exceeds World Class orienteering requirements
  • Leaps in and out of deep pits with one bound
  • Never mispunches with epunch stick
  • Can spot controls from 1000 meters
  • Can run through Dark Green
  • Give guidance policy to GOD

First Class Orienteer

  • Exceeds expected orienteering requirements
  • Must take running start to leap out of deep pits
  • Once a year mispunches
  • Can spot controls from far away
  • Can run though Medium Green
  • Talks with GOD

Second Class Orienteer

  • Meets expected orienteering requirements
  • Can only leap out of shallow pits
  • Sometimes states, "I know I punched that control"
  • Can spot controls from 50 meters
  • Can run through Light Green
  • Listens to GOD

Third Class Orienteer

  • Meets minimum orienteering requirements
  • Crashes into pit walls when attempting to leap out of them
  • Leaves epunch in for two beeps
  • Follows other orienteers to controls
  • Falls occasionally while running across rough open
  • Talks with animals

Fourth Class Orienteer

  • Fails to meet minimum orienteering requirements
  • Cannot recognize pits or depressions
  • Stabs self in finger when punching in at manual controls
  • Gets lost trying to find start area
  • Falls regularly while walking across open land
  • Talks to the trees after walking into them

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest Spring 2007

We completed the 25th Season of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) with a great Championship Meet at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on February 17th. The weather was great, the courses were challenging, and many enjoyed a picnic lunch while waiting for the awards ceremony.

The league had 227 registered participants. By the end of the Championships there had been 1084 starts. Both figures set a new record. The top teams were Middle School-Bremerton, JV Girls-EAS, JV Boys-Burlington-Edison, Varsity Girls-Lakeside, Varsity Boys-Thomas Jefferson. The top individuals were Elementary Boys-Mathew Rogers, Middle School Girls-Anne Wilkinson, Middle School Boys-Michael Klinge, JV Girls-Kelly Bauer, JV Boys-Joseph Spencer, Varsity Girls-Tori Borish, Varsity Boys-Huw Stradling.

11 WIOL participants will be participating in the US Interscholastic Orienteering Championships being held near Wilmington, DE on March 29 and April 1. I expect to see our participants at the top of the listings after the championship meet.

The current editor is finishing his promised 4 issues and a new editor is urgently needed. Editorship can be very rewarding and can expand your editorial and text formatting skills! The primary requirement is to have some computer skills. Past issues have been put together using everything from FrameMaker, and InDesign, to Microsoft Word. Templates are available and I promise lots of help from past editors.

Copyright 2007, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published four times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submissions for the next issue must be received by June 15, 2007. Send submissions to the next editor.

2007 Street Scramble season opens April 29 in U-District by Terry Farrah

Since 2003, Cascade has partnered with Meridian Geographics to put on a series of urban orienteering events each spring and summer. The series has expanded each year, with seven events planned for 2007.

[Image: Black and white photograph of two young male orienteers, Tyler Frederickson and Jake Reeder, standing in a park setting and looking closely at a map. Caption: Tyler Frederickson and Jake Reeder at a Green Lake control (photo by Brian Thompson).]
The season opener is the U-District Street Scramble on Sunday, April 29, at University Heights Center. This has been the most popular Street Scramble of the year, with 288 attending last year.

Street Scramble has a score-O format with 90-minute and 3-hour options. About 35 control locations are circled on a modified USGS map of a town or neighborhood. Each control has a point value. The object is to gather as many points as possible in the time limit.
Because of the risk of theft, control bags and epunch units are not used. Instead, participants answer a multiple-choice question about each control feature to prove they were there. Example:
Feature: Statue, NW corner of intersection Question: What is the date on the plaque at the base of the statue? A: 1973 B: 1989 C: 2001
Many Street Scramble regulars are Cascade members, but many others have never tried forest orienteering, even after doing Street Scramble for years.

This year a hybrid urban/park Street Scramble will be offered at Seward Park on Friday, June 22. Some controls will be in the park using conventional markers, and some will be on the neighborhood streets. We hope that by exposing Street Scramble regulars to park orienteering we will attract them to attend Cascade's other events. The Seward Park Street Scramble will start at 7pm.

Meridian Geographics, or "MerGeo", is myself (Terry Farrah) and Eric Bone. We are both Cascade members and, through MerGeo, we earn our living putting on navigation and running events full-time. Besides Street Scramble, MerGeo also puts on a sprint adventure race series (BEAST), a trail run series (Northwest Trail Runs), and, starting in 2007, a trail running relay called Six Foot. Also, MerGeo puts on some Street Scramble events in partnership with other organizations besides Cascade.
There are two reasons behind Cascade's partnership with MerGeo in putting on Street Scramble:
Street Scramble attracts orienteering newcomers who might not attend a forest orienteering event.

The partnership allows MerGeo to apply for grants and sponsorships designated for non-profit organizations. Funds from such grants and sponsorships benefit both Cascade and MerGeo, with Cascade's share earmarked for the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL).

The U-District Street Scramble starts at 10:00 a.m. on Sunday, April 29. Map issue is at 9:30, and check-in opens at 8:30. The start is at University Heights Center, University Way NE and NE 50th St. Pre-registration price for Cascade members is $10/adult, $5 ages 6-21. Day-of-event prices are $5 more for ages 13+. WMS Junior High Team Prepares to Defend National Orienteering Championship in Maryland. Gabrielle Lindquist and Rachel Ellis

In all our years of participating in orienteering events and after-school practices, we never dreamed of having the opportunity of competing in the Interscholastics. The competition has given us a goal. We now have something to work for and struggle to achieve greatness in. Training for this event has also been very inspiring. Every day that we meet with the others in our team, we learn something new about orienteering, and its many different symbols, rules, and tips to complete a course faster. With the competition just one week away, we are training as much as possible, and looking forward to traveling to Maryland and participating in an event that we will remember for years to come.

[Image: Black and white photograph of four young female orienteers standing together outdoors, smiling at the camera. Caption: The WMS Junior Team: Rachel, Dana, Jordan, and Gabrielle (photo by Eileen Breseman).]

We completed a full four months of meets with the Winter O/WIOL Championships at Point Defiance Park in Tacoma on February 17th. I would like to thank the eight course setters involved for great courses. It is not an easy job designing seven courses that will be challenging to all involved. I would like to thank Jana Dvorak, our meet director for all the meets. Jana made sure that all positions were manned and took care of any problems that arose very quickly and efficiently. Thank you to Ben Hall for coordinating the Epunch system and getting the results out quickly. THANK YOU to all the many volunteers who stepped forward to run the meets. Without YOU we couldn't have had such a successful season.

We have had to cancel the first two meets after the completion of the Winter O/WIOL season for differing reasons. The Army at Fort Lewis was very slow getting our permission request finalized even though we started before Thanksgiving. Once we received permission Mike Schuh and I went to the area to upgrade the map. We quickly discovered that it was going to take much longer than anticipated and it couldn't be done before the meet. Will we have a meet at Fort Lewis in the future? That is our hope, but there is one concern that has to be cleared up before we can allow our youth to participate. I will be working the Fort Lewis personnel to hopefully fix this concern.

Our second meet was cancelled, Night O at Lincoln Park, because we did not have a Meet Director or Course Setter within the time period that the board had determined to cancel a meet. We did not like having to cancel the meet. We need people to step forward and volunteer for these two positions. We have people who will help and mentor any volunteers for these positions. Please volunteer. The meet director directions are on the webpage and the course setter requirements will be sent to whoever volunteers. Because two meets were cancelled we had to cancel the Ultimate O Series as well. We hope that we do not have to cancel any more of our spring meets. We are working on land permission for all the meets. Check the website and as soon as we receive it the "pending land permission" will be removed and you will know the meet will commence. Of course we still have to have a Meet Director and Course Setter.

The Cell Phone O that was run by our young orienteers as a benefit for the WIOL participants going to the US Interscholastic Championships was a great success. Hamlin Park with its many trails was a challenge. "Which way do I turn? How many trails do I pass? Are YOU SURE, I don't see anything like that! Tennis Courts, you really shouldn't be over there, turn around." The two benefits, Hyak Lake and Hamlin, raised $445 for the participants. We are planning on having another Cell Phone O in the fall.

A reminder that whistles are required to be carried by all individuals and one individual in a group at all Cascade meets. We will provide you with one free whistle, but if you leave it at home we will ask for a $1 donation.

The board decided that we needed to get the Appreciation Coupons to our volunteers quicker. So the new procedure is that after you work for 1½ hours at a meet you are to go see the Meet Director who will issue you your Appreciation Coupon. This way if you work the early shift you can use your coupon that day. If you work the late shift you can use your coupon at the next meet. We will not be mailing Appreciation Coupons out any longer. I would like to thank Heidi Cusworth who has been our Appreciation Coupon Coordinator for the last few years for doing a great job.

Don Denbo has completed his agreed upon four issues of Bearing 315. We need a new editor. There are four issues a year. Don will help you get started. Please contact me or Don if you are interested in this position.
Please remember that volunteers are what make this club run. If you enjoy orienteering please volunteer at some meets during the year.

If you are a competitive orienteer a good thing to do after each meet is to mark your map showing your route selection between each control. This enables you to compare and discuss route selection with other orienteers who ran the same course, thereby analyzing your route selection. As you share experiences with other orienteers you will pick up new techniques and tactics and improve your own route selections. This is a good way to keep your mind thinking about route choices between meets and allows you to "run" a course any time you want. (February/March 1981 Bearing 315)
Practical marker hanging for course setters: A guide explaining how we do it.
I have not found this information written anywhere and think it could be useful in inducing orienteers who have not previously to try setting courses.

This is not about course design for there are many sources available discussing how to set courses and what the requirements are for the various age/sex classes. This writeup assumes that courses have been designed 2 weeks before a meet and suggests efficient ways of having them vetted and hanging the markers with minimal effort.

Using bright neon surveyor's tape, put a foot-long piece at the control site with the course and control number listed. For example, course 3F, #4 and course 4, #8. This allows the course vetter (at her convenience) to check all the locations in the field (not just on paper) with enough time to make suggestions for changes when necessary.
After the course vetter has approved the courses, get the control markers as soon as they are available. Assign control marker codes to each of the controls on the courses.
Prepare a master map putting all the courses' control circles on the single map with the control marker codes (e.g., 106) at the corresponding circle.

Divide up the map into sectors relative to where one would park vehicle when hanging, with perhaps 10-12 markers in each sector. For large areas where there are few controls, the sector may contain only 3 for example.

For each of these sectors, plan a route as you might for score-O or for a rogaine. The key is expending as little energy as you can while hanging the markers on each route. Returning to your vehicle to reload with the markers for the next sector is part of the plan.
In course order, make a master punch check list. You don't have to actually punch a punch card but can merely copy dots on a sheet of paper for each. These lists will go to the finish/checking crew.

Take the control markers and arrange them according to how each sector route resulted. I use plastic grocery bags to keep each sector separate. This is best done the night before you intend to actually hang the markers.
Now assume we are at morning (early) of the meet.

Taking a) your list of course controls - control codes and b) your master map hang the markers in each sector. This will be easy because by sorting them in bags and having surveyor's tape in the field, a quick look at the master map assures you that you have the correct one at each control point.
By returning to vehicle to reload with next sector's markers, you can assure any early-arriving meet staff that things are going well.
After all markers are set out, have course vetter (or prerunners) run each course.

Fuel up with the breakfast that you missed. Congratulate yourself for your hard work and prepare to field complaints from disgruntled competitors who won't blame themselves for their problems but must blame SOMEONE.

This procedure works well for standard local meets. Even a slow Oer such as I used to be able (before for my foot problem) to set out the 30 or so markers for a local C meet in time for the start. Naturally for other types of meets, one must adapt.

For A (national or international) meets, using a course controller's master control sheet such as we used for the 1997 A meet at Trollhaugen helps keep the course design team informed of who did what when. I believe that Don Denbo has copy of this sheet on his computer if anyone wants to see blank one.

When using electronic check systems such as SportIdent or Emit, step 6 above may be omitted unless one is super paranoid. Instead, one must program the electronic markers to start at the correct time with their own IDs for the meet. Would be good idea to make sure batteries are all good as well.
Going into more detail about using either SportIdent or Emit is beyond the scope of this guide.
Finally, there is matter of control pickup. At small parks, I found it easier to use sector technique myself.

With lots of volunteer help with control pickup, it is essential that someone be in charge of checking off the retrieved markers making sure that none were missed. Sorting them numerically into the storage box is a good way to double check that none were missed. Even in rainy times, it is best not to leave until all have been retrieved.
I hope this takes the mystery out of course design/control marker hanging and encourages new setters to give it a try.
Mark Hartinger (President-1984), updated by Jim Siscel-2007
